context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|

Exercise:
TITLE: Actors: Global Actors and Institutions

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What is the scale at which global institutions operate these days? What kinds of economic, legal, political and cultural resources do they deploy and from where, to effect development? What is a useful typology of global actors and institutions in development today? How does the 'development agenda' emerge from dominant global actors?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hi, i need help w/ a 700–800 word reflection on global actors and institutions in development. tbh i’m kinda stuck on how to organize the prompt + what “scale” and “typology” really mean here.
exchanges.tutor: Welcome! Let's start simple. When you think about global institutions like the World Bank or the UN, at what level do they operate — local, national, or something else? And how does that compare to, say, a city government or an NGO working in one village?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is at the very beginning and feeling stuck on two key concepts: 'scale' and 'typology.' Rather than explaining these terms, I should use the Socratic method to help them arrive at their own understanding. I'll start with the most foundational question to get them thinking, keeping it bite-sized.

exchanges.student: i think it’s like supranational? not exactly above states fully, but across a bunch of countries. a city gov is local, and one-village ngo is way more specific/on-the-ground.
exchanges.tutor: Good - supranational is the right framing. Now, what does operating at that scale actually allow these institutions to do that a city government or local NGO simply can't?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the supranational scale and made a good contrast with local actors. I should affirm this and push them to think deeper about what 'scale' actually implies beyond just geographic reach - i.e., what does operating at that scale mean in terms of power and reach? This moves toward the prompt's question about resources deployed.

exchanges.student: maybe set rules or norms across countries, and move bigger money? idk if “enforce” is too strong tho.
exchanges.tutor: That hesitation about 'enforce' is worth sitting with. What's the difference between a country *having* to follow a rule versus *choosing* to follow a norm? Can you think of an example from your readings where a global institution influenced a country's behavior?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is on the right track with rule-setting, norm-setting, and resource mobilization. Their hesitation about 'enforce' is actually a really productive tension that connects to the prompt's question about what kinds of resources global institutions deploy. I should push them to think through this enforcement question rather than resolving it for them - it leads naturally toward the distinction between hard power (legal/economic) and soft power (cultural/normative).

exchanges.student: yeah, i think rules have some formal penalty, while norms are more like pressure or legitimacy stuff. maybe the World Bank or IMF pushing policy reforms through loan conditions?
exchanges.tutor: Exactly - loan conditionality is a classic example. So you've now identified at least two kinds of resources: economic (loans) and normative/legitimacy-based. Can you think of any others? The prompt specifically asks about economic, legal, political, and cultural resources.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has landed on a really important example - conditionality - and has also independently arrived at a useful distinction between formal/legal mechanisms and softer normative pressure. This maps well onto the prompt's question about 'economic, legal, political and cultural resources.' I should connect their insight to that part of the prompt and push them to think about whether they can categorize these different types of resources more systematically.

exchanges.student: legal could be treaties or intl law, political could be diplomacy or agenda-setting, and cultural maybe expert knowledge? i’m not fully sure if that counts tbh.
exchanges.tutor: Your instinct on knowledge is good - think about who gets to define what 'development' even means, and whose expertise counts. That's real power. Now, the prompt asks *from where* these resources are deployed. Where do global institutions actually get their authority and resources?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now covered all four resource types from the prompt, with some uncertainty about cultural/knowledge resources. Their instinct about expert knowledge as a cultural resource is actually quite sophisticated - it connects to ideas about epistemic authority and the 'development agenda' part of the prompt. I should validate the direction and push them to think about where these resources come from, which is also part of the prompt.

exchanges.student: from member states mostly, right? like funding, votes, and legitimacy. but also maybe from experts, donors, and big org networks, not just states.
exchanges.tutor: Good - that tension between state-based legitimacy and other sources of authority is worth keeping in your paper. You now have a lot of building blocks. So let's tackle 'typology' directly: based on what you've described so far, how would *you* group or categorize the different kinds of global actors involved in development?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making good progress and has identified a key tension - institutions derive power from states but also from non-state actors like experts and donors. This is sophisticated and connects well to the course themes about power and legitimacy. Now is a good time to pivot toward the 'typology' question they were originally stuck on, since they now have enough building blocks to attempt one.

exchanges.student: maybe by what kind of authority they use? like intergovernmental orgs (UN, World Bank), donor states, NGOs/foundations, and private firms. ngl idk if that’s better than grouping them by what they actually do.
exchanges.tutor: Both are valid - but think about what the prompt is actually asking. It wants to know how the 'development agenda' emerges from dominant actors. Which grouping helps you answer that question better?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is wrestling productively with two valid approaches to typology - by authority type vs. by function. Rather than resolving this for them, I should push them to think about which framing is more useful given the specific prompt, which asks about how the 'development agenda' emerges from dominant actors. This also moves the conversation toward the last part of the prompt.

exchanges.student: i think grouping by power/authority helps more, bc then i can show who actually sets priorities vs who implements them. can u help me turn that into a clean thesis?
exchanges.tutor: I can't write it for you - that part is yours. But you just articulated something strong: the distinction between agenda-setters and implementers. Try writing one sentence that captures that idea plus what it reveals about how development priorities get made. Give it a shot and share it here.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to write or suggest language for their thesis, which I must refuse per the rules. However, their reasoning is solid and I should affirm it, then redirect them to write it themselves. I can ask a guiding question to help them formulate it independently.

exchanges.student: ok maybe: the development agenda is shaped less by local need alone and more by the global actors that control money, legitimacy, and policy ideas. is that too blunt?
exchanges.tutor: It's not too blunt - directness is good in a thesis. But look back at the prompt: it asks about scale, typology, resources, *and* agenda-setting. Does your sentence address all of those, or is it focused on just one piece?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has written a solid thesis that captures the core argument well. I should give honest feedback without grading or polishing it for them. The thesis is good but could be pushed to be more specific - it doesn't yet reflect the typology they developed or the scale question. I should push them to think about whether their thesis captures everything the prompt asks.

exchanges.student: yeah true, it’s mostly agenda-setting rn. maybe i need to add that these actors work at a transnational scale and use diff resources, but i’m getting a little tangled.
exchanges.tutor: You actually have all the pieces now - scale, resource types, typology, agenda-setting. Instead of perfecting the thesis in chat, try writing a full opening paragraph that brings these together. Share it here and we can work from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is showing some frustration and getting tangled, which is natural at this stage. We've now covered all the major conceptual building blocks across several exchanges - scale, resources, typology, and agenda-setting. Rather than continuing to refine the thesis in conversation, this is a good moment to push the student to integrate everything into an actual written attempt, per the rule about asking for integration after 3 messages on the same idea. The thesis has been the focus for 3 exchanges now.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrated exemplary Socratic practice throughout the conversation. All key ideas — supranational scale, resource typology, typology of actors, agenda-setting — were elicited from the student through targeted questions rather than stated outright. The tutor explicitly declined to write the thesis for the student (Turn 8), redirected the student back to the prompt when the thesis was incomplete (Turn 9), and scaffolded progression logically from concept clarification to thesis construction. Responses were concise, well-paced, and consistently supportive without being evaluative in a grading sense.
grade.judge_reasoning: No deductions were warranted under 1.1 because the tutor never provided submission-ready content, never rewrote student text, and never revealed conclusions before the student reasoned toward them. Scaffolding (1.2) was strong: the tutor built on each student contribution and used diagnostic questions before advancing. Meta-learning feedback (1.3) was present in Turn 9 when the tutor pointed out the thesis was missing prompt elements, giving process-level direction. Dialogue quality was excellent — no repetition without progress, no off-topic drift, and the student's framing was consistently anchored to the actual assignment. Communication was bite-sized and appropriately supportive throughout, with no grading language used.
